question: How many total descendants of Jacob entered Egypt according to the OSB tradition?

memoryAid.text: The OSB (Septuagint) counts 75, not the 70 of the Masoretic text. Remember 'SeptuagINT = 7 then 5': the Greek tradition Stephen quotes in Acts 7:14 gives 75 souls who went down into Egypt.

question: Which of these was NOT named among the sons of Jacob who came into Egypt in this chapter?

memoryAid.text: Joseph is not listed among those who 'came into' Egypt because he was already there, having gone before his brothers as a type of Christ who descends first into the place of exile and death to prepare deliverance for his people. The eleven are named as those who followed; Joseph is the forerunner.

question: A new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.

memoryAid.text: 'New king, no clue' — a new Pharaoh who did NOT know Joseph. Forgetting the man who saved Egypt sets the whole oppression in motion.

question: Which of the following was NOT one of the store cities the Israelites were forced to build?

memoryAid.text: Store cities: 'POR' — Pithom, On, Raamses. Goshen was where Israel LIVED, not what they built. Don't confuse home with the work site.

question: Name one of the three store cities the Israelites built for Pharaoh.

memoryAid.text: Store cities: 'POR' — Pithom, On (Heliopolis), Raamses. Pour your labor into POR.

question: Complete the verse: 'The more they afflicted them, the more they ___ and grew.'

memoryAid.text: Affliction could not stop God's promise to multiply Abraham's seed; persecution only made Israel grow. The Fathers see here the Church under the martyrs: the blood of the persecuted becomes seed, and the harder the world presses, the more the people of God increase.

question: What were the names of the two Hebrew midwives?

memoryAid.text: 'Shiphrah ships them, Puah pulls them' — two midwives who delivered babies and defied Pharaoh. Sounds like 'She-frah and Poo-ah.'

question: The midwives claimed the Hebrew women were too lively to wait for them to arrive in time.

memoryAid.text: 'Lively ladies, late midwives' — the Hebrew women delivered before the midwives could arrive. True.

question: Because the midwives feared God, He provided ___ for them.

memoryAid.text: The midwives feared God more than Pharaoh and chose life over the king's command, so God built them houses — He gave them families of their own. This is the pattern of holy disobedience to ungodly authority: those who guard life are rewarded with life, fruitfulness given to those who refuse to shed innocent blood.

question: Into what did Pharaoh command that every newborn Hebrew son be cast?

memoryAid.text: Picture the Nile turned into a drowning-pool for infants. The very river Egypt worshiped as life becomes a tomb — until one baby in a basket floats safely on it and undoes the whole decree.

question: How long was the infant Moses hidden by his mother?

memoryAid.text: 'Hidden a trimester' — three months, the length of a pregnancy's first stage. Moses was kept secret as long as a hidden pregnancy before he could be hidden no longer.

question: From what materials was the ark (basket) for baby Moses made?

memoryAid.text: Moses' little 'ark' of bulrushes and asphalt echoes Noah's ark sealed with pitch: both carry the one through whom God will save His people safely through deadly waters. The same Greek word for Noah's ark is used here, marking Moses as a new deliverer drawn through the flood of the Nile.

question: Why did Pharaoh's daughter go down to the river?

memoryAid.text: Picture the princess wading in to bathe and lifting a crying basket out of the reeds. The daughter of the man who decreed drowning becomes the one who pulls a Hebrew baby OUT of the water — God's irony in a single scene.

question: Who was paid to nurse her own child, Moses?

memoryAid.text: 'Jo-keb-ed got a paycheck' — Jochebed, Moses' own mother, was hired and PAID to nurse her own son. God turns Pharaoh's house into the funder of Israel's deliverer.

question: Moses went out to his brethren and saw their ___.

memoryAid.text: Though raised in Pharaoh's palace, Moses goes out to his brethren and identifies with their suffering rather than his privilege. Hebrews 11 praises this choice — to suffer with the people of God rather than enjoy sin's pleasures — as a foreshadowing of Christ, who leaves the Father's glory to share our affliction.

question: Where did Moses hide the Egyptian he had killed?

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses frantically kicking sand over a body, glancing left and right. The desert that should hide the deed cannot — the buried secret surfaces the very next day in a Hebrew's accusing question.

question: To what land did Moses flee after Pharaoh sought to kill him?

memoryAid.text: Moses runs to MIDian to hide in the MIDdle of nowhere. Midian = the wilderness exile where the shepherd-prophet is forged.

question: Moses helped his future wife's family by saving them from shepherds at the well.

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses, fresh off the run, driving off bullying shepherds so seven sisters can water their flock. The man who defended one Hebrew now defends strangers — and waters a wife's family into his future.

question: By which two names is Moses' father-in-law known?

memoryAid.text: 'JR' — Jethro/Reuel, two names for one priest of Midian. Reuel (Raguel) is the personal name; Jethro the title-name. Same man, two labels.

question: What was the name of Moses' firstborn son?

memoryAid.text: GERSHom = 'a stranger / sojourner there.' Hear 'GER' (Hebrew for stranger). Moses names his son for his own exile: 'I have been a stranger there.'

question: What does the name Gershom mean?

memoryAid.text: Gershom means 'a stranger there,' for Moses said he had been a sojourner in a foreign land. The people of God live as sojourners and pilgrims on the earth, our true home being the Kingdom; Moses names his son to confess that this world is not our resting place.

question: At the end of chapter 2, God remembered His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

memoryAid.text: God 'remembering' His covenant does not mean He forgot; in Scripture, to remember is to act on behalf of one's promise. The cries of Israel rise up, and God moves to fulfill the oath sworn to the patriarchs — the same faithfulness by which He remembers us in His Kingdom.

question: By what name is the mountain of God called where Moses saw the burning bush?

memoryAid.text: 'HOReb is where God said HELLO' to Moses — the mountain of God, also called Sinai. One mountain, two names.

question: The bush burned with fire but was not consumed.

memoryAid.text: The bush that burns yet is not consumed is a great image of the Theotokos in Orthodox tradition: she contains the divine Fire in her womb without being destroyed. The Unburnt Bush foreshadows the Virgin who bears God and remains whole, and it reveals a God who dwells in fire that gives life rather than annihilates.

question: Moses removed his cloak because he was standing on holy ground.

memoryAid.text: Not cloak — SANDALS. 'Holy ground, shoes off' — Moses removed his sandals, not his cloak. The feet that touch holy earth must be bare.

question: What did God command Moses to remove because he stood on holy ground?

memoryAid.text: 'Holy ground, shoes off.' Sandals come off the feet on holy ground — the same reverence the Church keeps before the holy places of God.

question: What was Moses' first response when God called to him from the bush?

memoryAid.text: 'Here I am' is the prophet's answer of total availability — the same word Abraham, Samuel, and Isaiah give when God calls. It is the posture of the saint: not 'what do you want?' but 'I am present and ready,' a self-offering to the will of God.

question: God identified Himself to Moses as the God of which three patriarchs?

memoryAid.text: Christ Himself cites this title to prove the resurrection: God is not the God of the dead but of the living, so Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob live to Him. The covenant name binds the whole story of salvation from the patriarchs to the Exodus into one continuous act of God.

topic: Land Flowing with Milk and Honey
question: The Promised Land is described as a land flowing with milk and ___.

memoryAid.text: Picture rivers of milk and golden honey running over the hills — abundance you can wade through. The land of slavery's whips gives way to a land that drips with sweetness, the rest God prepares for His people.

topic: Land Flowing with Milk and Honey
question: Which of these peoples was NOT listed as dwelling in the Promised Land?

memoryAid.text: The seven nations: Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites. 'CHAP GHJ' — no Philistines on that list.

topic: Land Flowing with Milk and Honey
question: Name one of the seven peoples dwelling in the land flowing with milk and honey.

memoryAid.text: Seven nations to dispossess: Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites — 'CHAP GHJ.' Pick any of the seven.

question: Complete Moses' question: 'Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh and bring the children of Israel out of ___?'

memoryAid.text: 'Who am I?' is the humility of the true prophet — not refusal of pride but awareness of his own smallness before the task. God's answer is not to flatter Moses but to promise 'I will be with you'; the worker is nothing, the One who sends is everything.

question: By what name did God reveal Himself to Moses (OSB rendering)?

memoryAid.text: In the OSB/Septuagint, God names Himself 'I AM THE EXISTING ONE' — He alone truly IS, the source of all being, while creation only exists by participation in Him. This is why the Church writes 'HO ON' (The Existing One) in Christ's halo on every icon: Jesus is the same I AM who spoke from the bush.

question: How does the OSB render the name God told Moses to give the children of Israel?
answer: The Existing One sent me to you
options: The Existing One sent me to you
The Lord of Hosts sent me

memoryAid.text: 'The Existing One' is the messenger-form of the divine Name: the One who simply IS sends Moses. The Fathers see in this name the unchanging, self-existent God who depends on nothing — and who, in Christ, enters time to draw existing-but-dying humanity back into true life.

question: How many times did Moses object or argue with the Lord before the Lord's anger was kindled?

memoryAid.text: 'Five fingers, five excuses' — Moses raised five objections, and the fifth is what angered the Lord. Count them off on one hand.

question: Which of these was NOT one of the three signs God gave Moses to perform?
options: Hand that becomes leprous and is healed

memoryAid.text: Three signs: Rod-to-serpent, Hand-to-leprous, Water-to-blood. The blossoming almond staff belongs to AARON later (Numbers 17), not to these three.

question: In the first sign, Moses' rod became a ___.

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses dropping his shepherd's staff and leaping back as it hisses to life as a serpent. The tool of guiding sheep becomes the sign of power over Egypt — and over the serpent itself.

question: In the second sign, Moses' hand became ___ when he drew it from his bosom.

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses pulling his hand out white and scaled with leprosy, then tucking it back and drawing it out whole. God shows He can strike and heal flesh in an instant — the same hand, sick then restored.

question: In the third sign, water from the river poured on dry land would become ___.

memoryAid.text: Sign three previews plague one: Nile water to BLOOD. 'Water-to-blood' is the dress rehearsal for the first plague that will strike Egypt's river.

question: Complete Moses' complaint: 'I am weak in speech and slow of ___.'

memoryAid.text: Moses pleads weakness of speech, yet God chooses the stammerer to confront the most powerful king on earth. God's strength is made perfect in weakness; He does not need eloquence but obedience, and He supplies Aaron's mouth so the glory remains His own.

question: Whom did God appoint to be Moses' spokesman before Pharaoh?

memoryAid.text: 'Aaron is the mouth, Moses is the message.' Aaron, the elder brother, speaks for slow-tongued Moses — A for Aaron, A for Articulate.

question: Complete God's words to Pharaoh: 'Israel is My ___ son.'

memoryAid.text: God calls Israel His firstborn son, claiming the nation as His own child. This sets up the justice of the final plague — Egypt enslaved and tried to kill God's son, so the firstborn of Egypt answers for it — and it points to Christ, the true Firstborn Son who leads a new Israel out of bondage.

question: Complete the warning to Pharaoh: 'If you refuse to let him go, indeed, I will kill your ___ son.'

memoryAid.text: Already in chapter 4 God announces the final plague: refuse My firstborn his freedom, and your firstborn will die. Measure for measure runs through Exodus — Pharaoh drowned Hebrew sons, so judgment falls on Egypt's sons. The threat foreshadows the Passover, where only the blood of the lamb spares the firstborn.

question: Who circumcised her son after the Lord confronted Moses on the journey?

memoryAid.text: Zipporah's swift circumcision of her son saves Moses' life, showing that the deliverer cannot lead the covenant people while neglecting the sign of the covenant in his own house. Before Moses can demand Pharaoh obey God, his own family must bear the seal of the promise — the mark of belonging to the LORD.

question: On the journey back to Egypt, the Lord met Moses and sought to kill him until Zipporah circumcised their son.

memoryAid.text: Picture the terrifying night camp: the Lord confronts Moses, and Zipporah grabs a flint and circumcises their boy on the spot, touching Moses' feet with it. A shocking scene that burns in the lesson — the covenant sign cannot be skipped.

question: Moses and Aaron told Pharaoh: 'Let My people go that they may hold a ______ in the wilderness.'

memoryAid.text: The first demand is not merely for freedom but for worship: 'that they may hold a feast.' In the patristic reading, Israel is delivered out of Egypt for the express purpose of serving God. Liberation and liturgy are inseparable; the goal of the Exodus is the worshiping assembly, which prefigures the Church gathered around the true Paschal feast.

question: Pharaoh responded, 'Who is the ______, that I should obey His voice?'

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh's question 'Who is the Lord?' is the defining posture of the prideful ruler who acknowledges no god above himself. In Egyptian theology Pharaoh was reckoned divine, so the plagues that follow are a direct contest revealing the LORD as the one true God. By the end, Egypt will know His name through judgment.

question: How did Pharaoh respond to the demand to let Israel go?
options: He demanded a tribute of gold
He agreed and then immediately repented

memoryAid.text: Picture Pharaoh sneering and piling MORE bricks onto already-bent backs. The first response to God's word is not relief but a crushing increase of the load.

question: Pharaoh ordered that the Israelites gather their own straw while keeping the same brick quota.

memoryAid.text: 'Same bricks, no straw, sore backs.' Same quota, but now they must find the straw themselves.

question: What building material were the Israelites suddenly required to gather for themselves while still meeting the same brick quota?

memoryAid.text: Straw for the bricks: 'No straw, same draw' — the quota is drawn up just as high as before.

question: Who were beaten when the brick quotas were not met?

memoryAid.text: See the Israelite foremen, caught in the middle, taking the lash for a quota no one could possibly meet without straw. The innocent middlemen bleed for Pharaoh's spite.

question: In Chapter 5, conditions for Israel grew worse before deliverance began.

memoryAid.text: A recurring pattern in salvation history: the darkness deepens just before the dawn. As the Cross precedes the Resurrection, so the worst affliction in Egypt comes right before God's mighty acts of deliverance. The increase of suffering is not abandonment but the threshold of redemption.

question: God reaffirms His covenant identity to Moses with the declaration, 'I am the ______.'

memoryAid.text: 'I am the LORD' (the divine Name, YHWH) frames the whole chapter of promises. This is the same Name revealed at the burning bush, the 'I AM' to which Christ alludes in John's Gospel. The promises that follow are guaranteed not by circumstances but by the very Being of God who keeps covenant.

question: How many major promises did God make to Israel in Exodus 6?

memoryAid.text: Seven promises — the number of completeness and covenant. God's deliverance is whole and perfect, lacking nothing.

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven promises God made in Exodus 6?
answer: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
options: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
I will give it to you as an inheritance

memoryAid.text: The seven 'I wills': bring OUT, rescue, redeem, take, be your God, bring IN, give as inheritance. Destroying Egypt's gods, though it happens, is not one of the seven covenant promises listed.

question: In the seven promises, God says 'I will ______ you with an outstretched arm' — the act tied to the price of deliverance.

memoryAid.text: To 'redeem' means to buy back, to ransom. God's redemption of Israel from Egypt prefigures the greater Redemption accomplished by Christ, who ransoms humanity from bondage to sin and death by His own outstretched arms upon the Cross. The Exodus language of redemption runs straight to Pascha.

question: Among the seven promises, God said 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God.'

memoryAid.text: This is the heart of the covenant formula: 'I will be your God, and you shall be My people.' It echoes through Scripture to the very last pages of Revelation, where God dwells with His people. The Exodus is ultimately about communion, God claiming a people for Himself.

question: Why did the Israelites not listen to Moses when he brought God's promises?
answer: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
options: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
Because they did not believe in God
Because Moses spoke in a foreign tongue

memoryAid.text: Picture a worker so exhausted by the whip that even good news bounces off him. Crushing bondage had hollowed out their hope; their spirits were too broken to hear.

question: The genealogy in Exodus 6 traces the lineage of which three sons of Israel?

memoryAid.text: First three sons in birth order: Reuben, Simeon, Levi. The list stops at Levi because the point is to establish the priestly line of Moses and Aaron.

question: Which of the following was NOT one of the three sons of Levi?

memoryAid.text: Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, Merari (G-K-M). Amram is a generation later — he is Kohath's son, not Levi's.

question: Which son of Levi was the father of Amram (and thus ancestor of Moses and Aaron)?

memoryAid.text: The priestly middle son: Kohath. 'Kohath the cornerstone' — through him comes Amram, then Moses and Aaron.

question: Who was the father of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

memoryAid.text: Amram + Jochebed = Aaron, Moses, Miriam. 'Amram's army of three.'

question: Who was the wife of Amram and mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

memoryAid.text: Jochebed — her name contains a form of the divine Name (Yah). The mother of the deliverer carries God's Name; she is the one who hid Moses in the ark of bulrushes.

question: Which of the following was NOT a child of Amram and Jochebed?

memoryAid.text: Children: Aaron, Moses, Miriam (A-M-M). Elisheba is Aaron's WIFE, not a sibling.

question: Who was the wife of Aaron?

memoryAid.text: Elisheba is the Hebrew form of 'Elizabeth.' The high priest Aaron's wife shares a name with the mother of John the Baptist — a wife linked to the priestly line.

question: Which of the following was NOT a son of Aaron?

memoryAid.text: Aaron's four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. Amram is Aaron's FATHER, not his son.

question: How many sons did Aaron have, as listed in Exodus 6?

memoryAid.text: Four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. 'NAEI' — say it like 'nay-eye.' Two would later perish offering strange fire; the priesthood continued through Eleazar and Ithamar.

question: Name the two sons of Aaron whose names begin with vowels and through whom the priesthood continued.

memoryAid.text: Nadab and Abihu died offering 'strange fire' before the Lord, so the Aaronic priesthood carried on through Eleazar and Ithamar. The continuity of the priesthood despite human failure foreshadows the unbroken priesthood fulfilled perfectly in Christ, the great High Priest.

question: God said, 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply My signs and My ______ in the land of Egypt.'

memoryAid.text: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is a theme the Fathers treat carefully: God's persistent mercy is the very thing a hardened heart turns to its own condemnation. The same sun that softens wax hardens clay. Pharaoh's resistance becomes the stage on which God's 'signs and wonders' display His glory to all nations.

question: Aaron was 83 years old when he and Moses stood before Pharaoh.

memoryAid.text: Aaron is the elder: 'Aaron Ate 3 more' — A-8-3. Moses was 80, Aaron three years older at 83.

question: How old was Moses when he stood before Pharaoh?

memoryAid.text: Moses' life splits into three 40s: 40 in Egypt, 40 in Midian, 40 leading Israel. He begins the Exodus at 80 — two forties down, one to go.

question: When Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, what did it become?

memoryAid.text: The rod becoming a serpent and devouring the magicians' serpents shows God's authority over the very symbol of Egyptian royal power, the cobra worn on Pharaoh's crown. The serpent, emblem of the ancient deceiver, is overcome and swallowed up — a sign that the powers of Egypt and of the enemy are subject to God.

question: The Egyptian magicians were able to replicate the sign of the rod becoming a serpent.

memoryAid.text: Picture the court magicians smugly tossing down their own rods, each writhing into a snake — only to watch Aaron's serpent gulp them all down. They could copy the trick but not the power.

question: What happened after the magicians' rods also became serpents?
answer: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
options: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
All the serpents turned back into rods

memoryAid.text: 'Aaron's snake ate the fake snakes.' God's sign devours the counterfeit.

question: What was the first plague God brought upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: The Nile was worshiped by Egypt as a life-giving god. By turning its waters to blood, the LORD strikes at the heart of Egyptian idolatry, exposing the false god as lifeless and deadly. Each plague targets a power Egypt trusted, declaring there is no god but the LORD.

question: Which of the following was NOT among the waters struck in the first plague?

memoryAid.text: Struck: Nile, Streams, Canals, Ponds, Reservoirs. The sea is not in the list — it was all of Egypt's fresh waters that turned to blood.

question: Which of the following was NOT a result of the first plague?
answer: The land was covered in darkness
options: The land was covered in darkness
The Egyptians could not drink the water

memoryAid.text: Results of plague 1: fish died, river stank, no drinking water. Darkness comes much later, as the ninth plague.

question: How many days did the first plague (water turned to blood) last?

memoryAid.text: Seven full days the Nile ran red. 'Seven days of dread by the river of red.'

question: The Egyptian magicians were able to imitate the first plague.

memoryAid.text: Picture the magicians turning a little dish of water red to save face — adding to the misery rather than relieving it. They could mimic the plague but never undo it.

question: What was the second plague God sent upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: The frog was associated with the Egyptian goddess Heqet, a deity of fertility and birth. By overwhelming the land with frogs, God again mocks the false gods of Egypt, turning a symbol of life into an unbearable plague. The judgment exposes the impotence of every idol.

question: Who stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt to bring up the frogs?

memoryAid.text: Aaron holds the rod and stretches his hand for the early plagues; Moses speaks, Aaron acts. 'Aaron's arm brings the swarm.'

question: Which of the following places did the frogs NOT invade?

memoryAid.text: Frogs got into Houses, Bedrooms, Ovens, and Kneading bowls — even your bed and your bread. No relief anywhere a person lives or eats.

question: The frogs entered the Egyptians' ovens and kneading bowls.

memoryAid.text: Imagine reaching into your bread dough and pulling out a slimy frog. The plague invades the most intimate places: your bed, your oven, your supper.

question: The magicians were able to bring frogs upon Egypt.

memoryAid.text: The magicians could ADD frogs but never SUBTRACT them. They could imitate the plague but could not remove it — only God can deliver.

question: After Pharaoh asked for relief from the frogs, what happened?
answer: Moses prayed and the frogs died
options: Moses prayed and the frogs died
Aaron drove them into the sea
The frogs returned to the Nile alive

memoryAid.text: Only prayer ends the plague: Moses prays, frogs die. The magicians could start it, but only God through Moses could stop it.

question: After the frogs died, how were the dead frogs gathered?

memoryAid.text: Picture rotting frogs piled into stinking heaps across the land, the whole country reeking. Even the relief leaves a stench — a reminder of the cost of Pharaoh's stubbornness.

question: Pharaoh kept his promise to let Israel go after the frogs were removed.

memoryAid.text: The moment relief comes, Pharaoh hardens his heart again. This pattern of repentance under pressure followed by renewed rebellion when comfort returns is the very portrait of a heart that will not truly turn to God. The Fathers see in Pharaoh a warning against false, fear-driven repentance.

question: What was the third plague, brought when Aaron struck the dust of the earth?

memoryAid.text: Third plague rises from the dust: lice/gnats. 'Dust to dust to bugs' — Aaron strikes the ground and the very dust becomes biting insects.

question: From striking what did Aaron bring forth the lice/gnats of the third plague?

memoryAid.text: From the dust man was formed, and from the dust the plague now comes. The lowliest element, the dust under their feet, becomes an instrument of God's judgment. This is the plague the magicians could not reproduce, for life from dust belongs to the Creator alone.

question: The magicians were able to duplicate the third plague of lice/gnats.

memoryAid.text: Plague three breaks the streak: the magicians fail. 'Three and done' — they could fake blood and frogs, but the gnats stopped them cold.

question: Unable to duplicate the third plague, the magicians said to Pharaoh, 'This is the ______ of God.'

memoryAid.text: 'This is the finger of God' is the magicians' confession of defeat. Christ later says He casts out demons 'by the finger of God' (Luke 11:20), linking His power over evil to this very moment. The phrase points to the Holy Spirit, the immediate working of God's power that no human counterfeit can match.

question: What was the fourth plague God sent upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Fourth plague: dog-flies / swarms of flies. 'Four for the flies that fill the skies.'

question: During the fourth plague, which region was spared from the swarms of flies?

memoryAid.text: Goshen is God's safe zone. 'Goshen = Go safe.' The land where Israel dwelt was kept untouched.

question: The fourth plague marks the first time God makes a clear separation between Israel and Egypt, protecting His people while the Egyptians suffered.

memoryAid.text: Beginning with the flies, God 'sets apart' Goshen so that no plague touches Israel. This separation reveals that the plagues are not random natural disasters but targeted acts of God who knows and protects His own. It prefigures the protection of the faithful at Passover and the ultimate distinction between those marked as God's people and the world.

question: After the fourth plague, Pharaoh immediately allowed Israel to leave Egypt without conditions.

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh offers compromises, suggesting Israel worship within Egypt or not go far, but then hardens his heart yet again. These half-measures mirror the temptations to compromise with the world rather than make a full departure. True deliverance requires a complete going-out, not a negotiated truce with Egypt.

question: Which of the following is the correct order of the second, third, and fourth plagues?

memoryAid.text: After blood (1): Frogs, Gnats, Flies — '1-2-3-4: Blood, Frogs, Gnats, Flies.' The magicians keep up through frogs, then fail at the gnats.

question: The fifth plague was a disease that struck Egypt's ______.

memoryAid.text: Plague 5 = liveSTOCK, and the stock (the animals) took a hit. The fifth plague struck the herds.

question: Which of the following animals was NOT named among those struck by the fifth plague?

memoryAid.text: The named five: Horses, Donkeys, Camels, Oxen, Sheep — 'Happy Donkeys Carry Old Sheep.' Goats are not on the list.

question: Israel's livestock died along with the livestock of Egypt.

memoryAid.text: God draws a clear line of separation between His people and Egypt, just as He will at Passover. This protection of Israel's herds prefigures the Church preserved through the plagues of this world; the Lord knows His own and shields them while judgment falls on the powers that enslave.

question: What did Moses toss into the air to bring about the sixth plague?

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses flinging a handful of furnace ashes skyward, the gray cloud drifting down over all Egypt and erupting into festering boils on every body it touches.

question: The sixth plague caused ______ to break out on both humans and animals.

memoryAid.text: Six = Sores. The sixth plague boiled up as boils on man and beast alike.

question: The magicians of Egypt were able to stand before Moses during the plague of boils.

memoryAid.text: The magicians who once mimicked Moses are now themselves afflicted and cannot even stand. The wisdom and power of Egypt collapse before the living God, showing that the counterfeit signs of the world's sorcery have no answer to the judgment of the Lord.

question: What unusual thing flashed within the hail of the seventh plague?

memoryAid.text: Imagine ice and fire falling together — flaming hailstones streaking down, fire running along the ground amid the storm. A sky at war with itself.

question: Which two crops were destroyed by the hail?

memoryAid.text: 'Flax and Barley Bit the dust' — the early B-crops (Barley) and the Flax were ripe and ruined; wheat and spelt came later and survived.

question: Which two crops survived the hail because they ripen later?

memoryAid.text: Wheat and Spelt were 'Late, so they Wait' — not yet grown up, they escaped the hail. Flax and barley, already ripe, were destroyed.

question: The wheat and spelt survived the hail.

memoryAid.text: Wheat and spelt ripen later — they had not grown up, so the hail passed them by. 'Late equals safe.'

question: Everyone in Egypt lost their servants and animals to the hail.

memoryAid.text: Those among Pharaoh's servants who feared the word of the Lord brought their people and animals indoors and were spared. Even in Egypt, the fear of God preserves life, foreshadowing how heeding God's warning shelters the obedient from the coming judgment.

question: During the hail, Pharaoh confessed, 'I have ______ this time.'

memoryAid.text: Picture Pharaoh shouting 'I have sinned!' over the roar of the storm — but the moment the hail stops, his face hardens again. A confession made of melting ice.

question: Pharaoh truly repented after confessing his sin during the hail.

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh's words were not repentance but fear of consequence; once relief came, he hardened his heart again. The Fathers see in this the difference between godly sorrow that leads to change and worldly regret that only seeks escape from punishment.

question: The eighth plague brought a swarm of ______ upon Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Eight = ATE. The locusts ate everything the hail had left. Plague eight is the plague that ate.

question: Which wind did God use to bring the locusts upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Locusts came on the EAST wind — and they came to EAT. East brings the feast of locusts.

question: The locusts ate everything that was left after the hail.

memoryAid.text: Picture the wheat and spelt that survived the hail now stripped bare — a living green tide of insects devouring the last surviving stalk of Egypt.

question: Pharaoh's servants asked, 'How long shall this man be a ______ to us?'

memoryAid.text: Picture Pharaoh's own court turning on him, pointing at Moses: 'This man is a trap we're caught in!' Even Egypt's officials see the snare before Pharaoh does.

question: During the locust plague, Pharaoh offered to let everyone in Israel leave.

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh tried to let only the men go, keeping families behind as hostages. His bargaining is the pattern of the enemy who offers partial freedom to keep a hold on God's people; true deliverance leaves no one in bondage.

question: The ninth plague covered Egypt in thick ______.

memoryAid.text: Nine = Night. The ninth plague turned Egypt into endless night for three days.

question: How long did the darkness cover the land of Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Three days of darkness — the same three days that will swallow up sea and tomb. Darkness for THREE, a number of burial and rising.

question: The Israelites also sat in darkness like the Egyptians.

memoryAid.text: While Egypt sat in palpable darkness, Israel had light in their dwellings. This is the great division between the children of light and the children of darkness; the same God who is darkness to those who resist Him is light to those who dwell with Him.

question: After the darkness, Pharaoh offered to let everyone go except their livestock.

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh keeps shrinking his offer: first only men, now everyone but the flocks. He wants to keep a claim — the livestock are his last leverage.

question: Moses replied that not one ______ of their livestock would be left behind.

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses planting his staff: 'Not one hoof stays behind!' Every animal is needed for sacrifice — God's people leave whole, taking everything with them.

question: After the darkness, one final plague still remained for Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Nine plagues down, ONE to go — the tenth and final blow, the death of the firstborn.

question: God declared that about ______ He would go out through the midst of Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Picture the deepest hour of the night, midnight, when the Lord Himself passes through Egypt. The darkest moment becomes the hour of deliverance — a foreshadow of the One who conquers death at the midnight of the world.

question: Israel was told to leave Egypt immediately, before the final plague struck.

memoryAid.text: Israel did not flee in panic; they waited for the Lord's instructions, which come in the next chapter as the Passover. Deliverance is ordered and liturgical, not chaotic; God prepares His people through the Passover meal before He leads them out.

question: Israel was promised they would leave Egypt carrying what?

memoryAid.text: Israel leaves with the silver and gold of Egypt, wages for generations of slavery. The Fathers see this 'plunder' as the rightful taking of what was owed, and a type of how the Church takes what is good from the world and offers it back to God in the tabernacle and worship.

question: What was required of the Passover lamb's condition?

memoryAid.text: The lamb without blemish is the type of Christ, the spotless Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. Only the flawless can stand in the place of the firstborn, for only the sinless Christ can offer Himself for the life of all.

question: What sex and age was the Passover lamb to be?

memoryAid.text: A MALE in his first YEAR, without blemish — 'a young male, in his prime, no flaw.' Three marks: male, year-old, perfect.

question: The Passover lamb was to be a female of the flock.

memoryAid.text: The lamb was MALE — a type of Christ, the firstborn Son. Male, year-old, unblemished.

question: On which day of the month was the Passover lamb to be set apart?

memoryAid.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Set apart on the 10th, slain on the 14th — four days proving it without blemish.

question: On which day of the month was the Passover lamb to be killed?

memoryAid.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Set apart on the 10th, slain on the 14th.

question: Where was the blood of the Passover lamb to be applied?

memoryAid.text: Picture the blood traced up the two side posts and across the top lintel — a doorway marked in the shape of a cross, the saving sign pointing up and out, never trampled below.

question: The Passover blood was to be placed on the threshold of the door.

memoryAid.text: Picture the blood on the two side posts and the lintel — never the threshold. You would never trample the saving blood underfoot; the cross-shape of doorway blood points up and out, never down to be stepped on.

question: Which three things made up the Passover meal?
answer: Lamb roasted with fire, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs
options: Lamb roasted with fire, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs
Boiled lamb, leavened bread, and sweet herbs
Roasted lamb, unleavened bread, and honey
Raw lamb, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs

memoryAid.text: Three for the meal: Roasted (by Fire), Flat bread (unleavened), Bitter herbs — 'Fire, Flat, and Bitter.' The fire of the cross, the sinless bread, the bitterness of bondage.

question: How was the Passover lamb to be cooked?

memoryAid.text: The lamb was roasted whole with fire, not boiled or eaten raw. The Fathers see the fire as the suffering of the cross and the unbroken roasting as a sign that not a bone of Christ would be broken, the Lamb consumed entire by the fire of His Passion.

question: The Passover bread was to be leavened.

memoryAid.text: The bread was unleavened, for there was no time to let it rise and leaven is a symbol of corruption and sin. The Church eats the sincerity and truth of unleavened bread, for Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us, the bread without the leaven of malice.

question: Along with the lamb and unleavened bread, the meal included bitter ______.

memoryAid.text: The bitter herbs recall the bitterness of slavery in Egypt. Even in the feast of deliverance, the people taste the bitterness from which they were saved, remembering that redemption is bought through suffering and remembered with sober gratitude.

question: Of this feast God declared, 'It is the Lord's ______.'

memoryAid.text: Passover, or Pascha, means the 'passing over' of the destroyer. For the Church this is fulfilled in the Pascha of Christ, who by His own blood causes death to pass over us and passes us from death into life.

topic: Tenth Plague: Death of the Firstborn
question: Who died in the tenth and final plague?

memoryAid.text: Picture a single cry rising from every Egyptian house at midnight — from the palace of Pharaoh to the prison cell, the firstborn of each home struck down. No house untouched but those marked with blood.

topic: Tenth Plague: Death of the Firstborn
question: Who was spared from the death of the firstborn?

memoryAid.text: Only the blood on the door saved the house, not effort or merit. So too only the blood of Christ shelters us from death; the destroyer passes over all who are marked by the Lamb, a salvation given by grace through the sign of sacrifice.

topic: Tenth Plague: Death of the Firstborn
question: Pharaoh's own son died in the final plague.

memoryAid.text: Even the throne was not spared — Pharaoh's firstborn, the heir of Egypt, lay dead. The hardest heart in Egypt finally felt the cost of resisting God.

question: How many years did Israel sojourn in Egypt and Canaan?

question: About how many men left Egypt, not counting women and children?

question: The figure of about 600,000 leaving Egypt counted only the men, not the women and children.

question: The Passover lamb was set apart on the 10th day and killed on the 14th day of the month.

memoryAid.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Four days under inspection prove the lamb is without blemish before it is slain.

question: In Exodus 13, every firstborn belongs to God.

memoryAid.text: Because the Lord spared Israel's firstborn at the Passover, every firstborn is consecrated to Him. The Fathers see in this a foreshadowing of Christ, the Firstborn of all creation and the Firstborn from the dead, who sanctifies humanity to God.

question: How many days did the Feast of Unleavened Bread last?

memoryAid.text: Seven days, a full and perfect week of unleavened bread — seven for completeness.

question: God led Israel by the shortest route to Canaan.

question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the day?

question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the night?

memoryAid.text: The pillar of cloud by day and fire by night is God's abiding guiding presence with His people. The Fathers see here the unfailing presence of God who leads His Church through the wilderness of this world toward the promised land.

question: What did Moses bring with him out of Egypt, fulfilling an ancient promise?

question: Whose promise was fulfilled when Moses carried bones out of Egypt?

Abraham's, that he would be buried in Canaan

question: Where did Israel camp before the sea?

question: Israel was confident when Pharaoh approached at the sea.

question: The Egyptians crossed the sea safely behind Israel.

memoryAid.text: The waters returned and covered Pharaoh's host so that not one remained. The Fathers see the drowning of the Egyptians as the destruction of sin and the demonic powers in the waters of Baptism, which Israel passes through unharmed.

question: What happened to Pharaoh's army at the Red Sea?

They turned back to Egypt unharmed
They crossed and pursued Israel into the wilderness
They were struck blind and wandered off

question: Complete the opening of the Song of Moses: 'Let us sing to the Lord, for he is very greatly glorified: horse and rider he has thrown into the ____.'

memoryAid.text: The Song of Moses is the first ode of the Church's canon of hymns, sung at the dawn of Israel's salvation. It celebrates the victory of God over the powers of Egypt, prefiguring the triumph of Christ over death.

question: Complete the verse: 'He was to me a helper and protector for salvation: this is my God, and I will ____ him.'

memoryAid.text: Helper, Protector, Salvation — then 'glorify': God acts, and we respond with glory.

question: Complete the verse: 'The Lord bringing wars to nought, the Lord is his ____.'

memoryAid.text: The Lord brings wars to nought — His very name spells the end of war.

question: What title is given to Miriam in the Song of Moses?

memoryAid.text: Miriam the prophetess — Moses' sister speaks and sings for God.

question: Miriam led the women with dance and what instrument?

memoryAid.text: Picture Miriam at the head of a line of women, timbrels ringing and feet dancing on the far shore while Egypt lies drowned behind them.

question: The people found fresh, sweet water when they arrived at Marah.

memoryAid.text: Marah means 'bitter' — the name itself tells you the water could not be drunk.

question: What did Moses cast into the bitter waters of Marah to make them sweet?

memoryAid.text: The Fathers see the Cross at Marah: bitter water made sweet by a tree thrown in, as the bitterness of death is made sweet by the wood of the Cross.

question: Complete the verse: 'I am the Lord thy God that ____ thee.'

memoryAid.text: At Marah the Lord reveals Himself as the One who heals. The Fathers see Christ the Physician, who heals not only bodies but the bitterness of fallen human nature through the sweetening wood of the Cross.

question: How many springs of water did Israel find at the oasis of Elim?

question: How many palm trees were at the oasis of Elim?

memoryAid.text: Twelve springs, seventy palms — twelve like the Apostles, seventy like the disciples Christ sent out.

question: At Elim Israel found twelve springs of water and how many palm trees?

memoryAid.text: Picture a green island in the desert: twelve gushing springs and seventy palm trees swaying — a resting place after the bitterness of Marah.

question: On what day did Israel come to the Wilderness of Sin?

options: The fifteenth day of the second month
The first day of the first month
The fortieth day after the Passover
The seventh day of the third month

memoryAid.text: Fifteenth of the second month — exactly one month after leaving Egypt.

question: Israel reached the Wilderness of Sin about one month after leaving Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Left Egypt on the fifteenth, hungry in the wilderness on the fifteenth a month later — one month to the day.

question: Israel journeyed from Elim into which wilderness, where the manna was given?

memoryAid.text: From Elim's springs to the Wilderness of Sin — the place-name 'Sin' is from Sinai, not the word for transgression.

question: What did the people say they had sat beside in Egypt when they complained of hunger?

question: In chapter 16 Israel went from singing God's praises to complaining about hunger.

memoryAid.text: From the triumphant Song of the Sea to murmuring over bread, Israel reveals the fickleness of the human heart. The Fathers warn that the soul delivered from sin must still be fed daily, lest it grumble and look back to its old slavery.

question: When did the manna appear each day?

memoryAid.text: Bread in the morning, meat at night — manna with the dew, quail with the dusk.

question: When did the quail appear?

memoryAid.text: Quail in the evening, manna in the morning — flesh by night, bread by day.

question: What does the word 'manna' mean?

memoryAid.text: Seeing the strange bread, Israel asked 'What is it?' — and that question became its name. The Fathers see the manna as a type of the Eucharist, Christ the Bread of Life come down from heaven, a mystery that surpasses understanding.

question: Israel could store leftover manna for the next day without it spoiling.

memoryAid.text: Manna kept overnight bred worms and stank, except the portion gathered before the Sabbath. The Fathers see a lesson in daily dependence on God, echoed in 'give us this day our daily bread' and in the daily reception of Christ the living Bread.

question: How much manna did Israel gather on the sixth day?
options: The same as every other day

question: According to Exodus 16:8, against whom was Israel's murmuring truly directed?

question: According to Exodus 16:8, the Lord gives flesh in the evening and what in the morning?

question: How much manna was each person to gather according to Exodus 16:16?

question: According to Exodus 16:31, what was the taste of the manna like?

question: Manna was white and like the seed of what plant in appearance?

memoryAid.text: Picture the desert floor at dawn dusted with tiny white seeds like coriander, glittering with the morning dew — bread from heaven scattered on the ground.

question: For how many years did Israel eat manna?

memoryAid.text: Forty years of wandering, forty years of manna — bread from heaven the whole way.

question: Israel ate manna for forty years until they reached the borders of Canaan.

memoryAid.text: Manna fed Israel for forty years until they entered the promised land. The Fathers see the Eucharist as the manna of the new Israel, sustaining the Church through her pilgrimage until she enters the Kingdom.

question: Which of these is NOT something that happened in the Wilderness of Sin?

options: Water flowed from a struck rock
A double portion was gathered on the sixth day

memoryAid.text: Chapter 16 is bread and quail; the rock that gives water comes later, in chapter 17.

question: At what place did Israel camp when there was no water, leading to the water from the rock?

question: Moses struck the rock to bring forth water.

question: At which mountain did God tell Moses he would stand on the rock to bring forth water?

question: What two names did Moses give the place where the people tested the Lord?

question: What do the names Massah and Meribah mean?

question: Who led Israel's army into battle against Amalek?

memoryAid.text: Joshua (Hebrew Yehoshua) bears the same name as Jesus (Yeshua). The one who leads Israel's warfare against Amalek shares the Savior's name, foreshadowing Christ who leads His people in victory over the enemy.

question: What happened to the battle whenever Moses raised his hands?

memoryAid.text: Moses on the hill with arms outstretched is a living image of the Cross: while his arms form the cross-shape, Amalek falls. Aaron and Hur holding up his tiring arms show that victory comes only through the Cross, upheld by the Church.

question: Who went up to the top of the hill with Moses during the battle with Amalek?

question: How did Aaron and Hur help Moses keep his hands raised?

put a stone under him and held his hands
sat him on a stone and held up his hands

question: What name did Moses give the altar he built after defeating Amalek?

question: Who came to visit Moses in the wilderness, bringing his wife and sons?

question: Jethro was an Egyptian priest.

question: Of what land was Jethro a priest?

question: What were the names of Moses' two sons?

question: What does the name of Moses' son Eliezer mean?

question: Jethro advised Moses to appoint rulers over groups of what four sizes?

question: Why did Jethro advise Moses to appoint judges over the people?

To collect taxes for the tabernacle
To choose a successor for Moses

memoryAid.text: Jethro's counsel to share leadership prefigures the ordered hierarchy of the Church, where bishops appoint presbyters and deacons so the burden of ministry is shared and the whole body is served.

question: In which month after leaving Egypt did Israel arrive at the wilderness of Sinai?

question: God said He bore Israel out of Egypt on what?

question: Which of the following did God NOT call Israel at Sinai?

question: God said Israel would be His special what, above all peoples?

question: At Mount Sinai everyone could freely approach and touch the mountain.

question: Which of the following was NOT among the signs that appeared on Mount Sinai?

question: What loud sound, besides thunder, signaled God's presence on Sinai?

question: The whole mountain quaked greatly when the Lord descended on Sinai.

question: What is the first commandment?

question: What is the eighth commandment?

question: The tenth commandment forbids coveting your neighbor's possessions.

question: The people asked God to keep speaking to them directly from the mountain.

question: Frightened by God's voice, whom did the people ask to speak to them instead?

question: What did God forbid in the construction of the altar's approach?

memoryAid.text: No carved stones and no steps — keep the altar plain and the approach humble. 'No steps' to keep modesty at the altar.

question: On what mountain was Moses when the people made the golden calf?

memoryAid.text: Sinai is also called Horeb, the mountain of God where Moses first met the burning bush. While Moses ascends to receive the Law of life, the people below fashion an idol of death. The pattern recurs in salvation history: the true Mediator goes up to God while the people fall away, until Christ the new Moses ascends and does not fail.

question: Who was on the mountain with Moses, having gone up partway?

memoryAid.text: Joshua (Yeshua) is the Hebrew form of the name Jesus. The one who goes up the mountain with the lawgiver bears the very name of the Savior, foreshadowing the One who leads the people into the Promised Land.

question: What did the people ask Aaron to do?
answer: Make gods to go before them
options: Make gods to go before them
Build an altar to the Lord

memoryAid.text: The people demand a god they can see and control, rejecting the unseen God who led them by cloud and fire. This is the root of idolatry: the refusal to wait on the living God. The Church Fathers see in this impatience the same restlessness that the Eucharist heals, where Christ Himself goes before His people.

question: What material did the people use to make the golden calf?

memoryAid.text: Picture the people tearing the gold rings from their own ears and their children's ears, throwing their family treasure into the fire to forge a dead calf. The same ears that should have listened to God's voice surrendered their gold to make a mute idol.

question: Aaron opposed the people and refused to make the calf.

memoryAid.text: Aaron AIDED, he did not avoid. He collected the gold and fashioned the calf himself. Remember: the high priest fell, so the people fell with him.

question: What excuse did Aaron give Moses for the calf?
answer: I cast the gold into the fire, and this calf came out
acceptableAnswers: i cast it into the fire and this calf came out
i threw the gold in the fire and out came this calf
the calf came out of the fire
i cast it into the fire and out came this calf

memoryAid.text: Aaron shrugs and claims the calf just walked out of the furnace on its own, as if the fire gave birth to a golden cow. The absurd excuse exposes how sin always blames the circumstances instead of the will.

question: Which group stood with Moses when he called, 'Who is on the Lord's side'?

memoryAid.text: The tribe of Levi rallies to the Lord and is set apart for priestly service because of their zeal. Their willingness to stand against even their own kin becomes the basis of their consecration. The Fathers see here that true priesthood is born of zeal for God's holiness.

question: About how many men died after the golden calf incident?

memoryAid.text: Calf-craze cost 3,000. Contrast Pentecost (Acts 2:41): when the Spirit came on the Church, about 3,000 were ADDED. The Law without the Spirit kills 3,000; the Spirit gives life to 3,000.

question: About how many descendants of Jacob entered Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Seventy-five souls entered Egypt according to the Septuagint and Acts 7:14, the tradition the Orthodox Study Bible follows. A small remnant goes down into Egypt and comes out a vast nation, a pattern of death and resurrection, of the few becoming many through God's promise.

question: How old was Aaron when he stood before Pharaoh?

memoryAid.text: Aaron is 83, three years older than Moses (80). The high priest is the elder brother, yet he serves the prophet's word.

question: How many days did the plague of darkness last?

memoryAid.text: Three days of darkness so thick it could be felt. The three days of darkness over Egypt foreshadow the three days Christ lay in the tomb, the darkness before the dawn of deliverance and resurrection.

question: How long did Israel dwell in Egypt?

memoryAid.text: 430 years in Egypt (Ex 12:40). On the very day the 430 years ended, the Lord brought them out. God keeps His appointed times to the very day.

question: About how many men left Egypt in the Exodus?

memoryAid.text: About 600,000 men on foot left Egypt, besides women and children. From 75 souls to 600,000 men: the seed of Abraham multiplied beyond counting, as God promised.

question: How many springs of water were at Elim?

memoryAid.text: Twelve springs at Elim, one for each tribe of Israel, and a type of the twelve apostles, the wells of living water for the new Israel. The number twelve marks the fullness of God's people.

question: How many palm trees were at Elim?

memoryAid.text: Seventy palm trees at Elim, recalling the seventy elders and prefiguring the seventy disciples Christ sent out to the nations (Luke 10). Twelve springs for the tribes, seventy palms for the nations: Israel and the world both find rest at the oasis.

question: How many days and nights was Moses on Mount Sinai?

memoryAid.text: Forty days and forty nights on Sinai, the number of testing and preparation. Forty for Moses, forty for Elijah, forty for Christ in the wilderness: the mountain of God demands the full forty.

question: The twelve springs and seventy palm trees were located at what place?

memoryAid.text: Elim, the oasis of 12 springs and 70 palms. Remember Elim by its abundance: twelve and seventy, tribes and nations, all finding water and shade.

question: Where was the tent of meeting (tabernacle of testimony) placed?
options: In the center of the camp
At the entrance to the camp

memoryAid.text: After the golden calf, the tent of meeting is pitched outside the camp, signifying the breach between a sinful people and the holy God. To seek the Lord one had to go OUT, away from the corrupted multitude. This anticipates Christ, who suffered outside the gate (Heb 13:12), that He might sanctify the people.

question: Everyone entered the tent of meeting together with Moses.

memoryAid.text: Moses went IN, the people stayed AFAR. Only the mediator entered while the people worshiped from the doors of their tents. One man stands between God and the multitude, as Christ does for us.

question: Which young man remained in the tent of meeting?

memoryAid.text: Joshua stays in the tent, never departing from the place of God's presence. The bearer of the name Jesus is the one who dwells where God dwells.

question: What did Moses request of God, saying 'Show me Your...'?

memoryAid.text: Moses asks to see God's glory, the deepest longing of the soul for God Himself. The Fathers, especially St. Gregory of Nyssa, see Moses' ascent as the model of endless growth into God: the more we behold, the more we desire. This longing is fulfilled in Christ, in whom the glory of God shines in the face of a man.

question: Moses was permitted to see God's face directly.

memoryAid.text: No man may see God's face and live (Ex 33:20). The unveiled glory of God would consume mortal flesh. Yet in the Incarnation, God grants what was impossible to Moses: in Christ we behold the glory of God in a human face (2 Cor 4:6), and live.

question: What was Moses permitted to see as the Lord passed by?
answer: God's back parts / glory passing by
options: God's back parts / glory passing by
God's face shining like the sun

memoryAid.text: Hidden in the cleft of the rock and covered by God's own hand, Moses is allowed only to glimpse God's back as He passes. The Fathers say the rock that shelters him is Christ, and to see God's 'back' is to follow after Him, for we know God by following where He has gone.

question: How many new stone tablets did Moses cut for the renewed covenant?

memoryAid.text: Two tablets, twice given. The first set Moses broke; the second he carved himself at God's command. Two tablets, two tables of the Law: love of God and love of neighbor.

question: How many days and nights was Moses on the mountain renewing the covenant?

memoryAid.text: Forty: the number of testing and preparation. Forty days for Moses, forty days of rain in the flood, forty years in the wilderness, forty days of Christ's fast. Forty always precedes a covenant or a new beginning.

question: Moses ate bread and drank water during the forty days on the mountain.

memoryAid.text: Moses neither ate bread nor drank water for forty days, sustained by the presence of God alone. This total fast prefigures Christ's forty-day fast and is the pattern for the Church's own Great Fast of Lent. Man does not live by bread alone, but by the word of God.

question: What happened to Moses' face after he spoke with God?

memoryAid.text: Moses' face shone with reflected glory from speaking with God, yet that glory faded and had to be veiled. St. Paul contrasts this in 2 Corinthians 3: the glory of the old covenant was fading and veiled, but in Christ we behold the unveiled and abiding glory, being transformed from glory to glory.

question: Why did Moses put a veil over his face?
answer: Because the people feared to approach him
options: Because the people feared to approach him
To hide his identity from enemies
To protect his eyes from the sun
Because God commanded him to hide the tablets

memoryAid.text: The people were afraid of the divine glory shining on Moses, so he veiled it. St. Paul reads the veil as the obscurity that still lies over the old covenant when read apart from Christ. When one turns to the Lord, the veil is taken away, and we behold His glory with unveiled face.

question: Of what material was the altar of burnt offering made?

memoryAid.text: Acacia wood (incorruptible) plus bronze (judgment by fire). The altar where sacrifice is consumed is wood that cannot rot, sheathed in the metal of fiery judgment, prefiguring the Cross that cannot be corrupted.

question: The bronze laver was made from the mirrors of which women?
answer: The women who served/fasted at the tabernacle entrance
acceptableAnswers: the women who served at the tabernacle entrance
women at the door of the tabernacle

memoryAid.text: The women melted down the very mirrors they used to admire themselves, and from that vanity-glass came the basin where the priests washed clean. Their surrendered self-regard literally became the means of purification.

question: What was the length of the tabernacle court?

memoryAid.text: The court is 100 long by 50 wide, a perfect 2-to-1 rectangle. Picture a hundred-cubit hall, twice as long as it is wide.

question: What was the width of the tabernacle court?

memoryAid.text: Width 50, length 100. The width is exactly half the length, a 2-to-1 ratio for the sacred enclosure.

question: The tabernacle used silver for every single furnishing.

memoryAid.text: Three metals, not one: Gold, Silver, Bronze (G-S-B). Gold for the holiest things, silver for the bases, bronze for the courtyard. The glory decreases as you move outward from God's presence.

question: How much gold was collected and used for the holy vessels?

memoryAid.text: Gold = 29 talents, 720 shekels. Gold is the least by weight but the most precious, reserved for the vessels that minister directly before God.

question: How much silver was collected for the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: Silver = 100 talents, 1775 shekels. The silver came from the census 'ransom' of each man, so it equals the largest count, 100 full talents.

question: How much brass/bronze was collected for the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: Bronze = 70 talents, 1500 shekels, used for bases, pins, and altar vessels. Seventy talents, like the seventy elders and seventy nations: bronze is the metal of the outer court and the wider world.

question: The blue, purple, and scarlet materials were used to make what?
options: The veil of the tent only

memoryAid.text: The colors blue, purple, and scarlet adorn the high priest, the same colors that veil the holy place. The high priest wears the colors of heaven, royalty, and blood, for he mediates between God and the people. Christ our High Priest fulfills all this in His own person.

question: How many men aged twenty and upward passed the survey/census?
acceptableAnswers: six hundred three thousand five hundred fifty

memoryAid.text: 603,550 passed the census. Each contributed a half-shekel of silver, which is why the silver totaled exactly 100 talents. The number redeemed becomes the number that builds the dwelling of God.

question: Which of the following was NOT among the metals collected for the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: Three metals only: Gold, Silver, Bronze. Iron, the metal of weapons and war, has no place in the dwelling of the God of peace.

question: On what date was the tabernacle erected?
answer: The first day of the first month of the second year
options: The first day of the first month of the second year
The tenth day of the seventh month
The fifteenth day of the first month
The first day of the third year

memoryAid.text: The tabernacle rose on the first day of the first month, exactly one year after the Exodus began. A new creation: as the first day saw light, so the first day of the year sees God come to dwell among His people. The ordering of sacred time marks the ordering of the world around God's presence.

question: About how long had the Israelites been out of Egypt when the tabernacle was erected?

memoryAid.text: One year out of Egypt, and God comes to dwell. It took a year of wandering, law, and even the calf, before the people were ready for God to tabernacle among them.

question: What was the FIRST step in setting up the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: Order: Erect, Furnish, Wash, Anoint. First the house, then its contents, then the cleansing of the priests, then the anointing that consecrates. Structure before service.

question: What was the LAST of these steps in consecrating the tabernacle and priesthood?

memoryAid.text: Anointing comes last, sealing the priests for service. The anointing oil is the type of the Holy Spirit; the very word Christ (Messiah) means 'Anointed One.' What is last in the order is the crown of the whole: consecration by the Spirit.

question: What filled the tabernacle when it was completed?

memoryAid.text: The glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle, the same glory that will fill the Temple and that finally dwells bodily in Christ. The tabernacle filled with glory is the goal of the whole book of Exodus: God comes to dwell with His people. 'The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us' (John 1:14).

question: Moses was able to enter the tabernacle after God's glory filled it.

memoryAid.text: Moses could not enter because the glory of the Lord filled the tent. Even the great mediator is barred when the fullness of glory descends. This shows that no creature can stand in the unmediated presence of God, until Christ opens the way for us to enter the true Holy of Holies.

question: What was the function of the cloud over the tabernacle?
options: It provided rain for the camp
It marked the place of sacrifice
It hid the camp from enemies

memoryAid.text: Cloud UP, move out. Cloud DOWN, stay in town. When the cloud ascended, Israel traveled; when it rested, they stayed. God's presence sets the pace of the pilgrim people.

question: Israel traveled even when the cloud remained resting over the tabernacle.

memoryAid.text: They moved ONLY when the cloud lifted. To march without the cloud would be to journey without God. The Christian life moves only when the presence of God leads.

question: When the cloud ascended from the tabernacle, what did Israel do?
answer: They set out on their journey
options: They set out on their journey

memoryAid.text: Cloud rises, camp rises. Ascended cloud means 'move along'; descended cloud means 'stay put.' The lifting of the cloud is the marching order.
